speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Victor Lewis. OK, go ahead, Vera. Whenever you're ready. You can look at her. And OK, whenever you're ready, Vera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Lewis we were talking about the announcement, about the evacuation of the ghetto. Is that what it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deportation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Deportation from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to mention that this was not the first deportation, between-- there were-- there were other deportations, too. In June, there was a deportation of maybe 10,000 Jews. And in July, there was a deportation, also, from Cracow ghetto. But in October, was the biggest deportation of ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you a question?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people-- approximately, how many people were living in the ghetto at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, ghetto was the-- in the ghetto was always the same amount of people. But so we were cleaning up. They were deporting people out. And they were letting in other Jews, who were living on outskirts of other cities. And the-- the way that they put all Jews together, otherwise, they wouldn't be able to catch the all Jews. So the way they did, they pulled all Jews into the ghetto. Then they made a deportation. They clear out a little bit of space. And they ship-- put in other Jews from other little towns. But amount of people was always the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how much? Do you have any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell you. It was a plenty amount. If-- if-- if in one room, there were sleeping 6, 7, 8, and sometimes 10 people, so you can imagine how many Jews were there. There was plenty Jews in-- in-- in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I said the deportation were before. But I'm coming to deportation of October 28, because I was in-- involved in it, with my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said before, that I had the bescheinigung, papers that I'm working for SS, Waffen SS, as auto mechanic. And was-- I was 100% sure that those papers would help me where-- wherever I going to show the paper off. On this day, there was an announcement that the ghetto will be liquidated. And my father and my mother and my sister were-- we hide them in this building, where we live, and put them on-- in hiding, so nobody could find them. When we heard, my brother and me, when we heard about there will be deportation and the ghetto will be liquidated, so we went home. As I-- my brother run home-- and I-- I approached to the house, the whole family of mine was already in-- in the room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I said, why did you came out? Why did you-- didn't stay in the-- in the place where you were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, the SS people came in and throw us on the street. And on the street were staying the big SS people, the-- the officers. And I handed them the-- the bescheinigung, the SS bescheinigung, that I am a-- a auto mechanic for SS [GERMAN]. It was very, very important for German. And one of those officers took the-- the bescheinigung and tore it. And he said, you're going to go together with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went on Plac Zgody. And here, I was approved and nothing was helping, even the-- even the piece of paper who came with the-- with the hakenkreuz on it, with all kind of stamps from the-- from the Nazis and from the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing was helping me. They wanted to clean up. They clean up even those people who they were very needed for them to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he tore up the papers. And they put us on the Plac Zgody. I say, Plac Zgody, with my father, with my brother, sister, and-- and my mother. I met, sitting on the floor, my wife's mother, with two sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that, if I going to stay with them, then I wouldn't be able to escape. Because, in my mind, it was always, if they're going to catch me someplace, I-- I would have run.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I pass the family of my wife and went deeper into the Plac Zgody. And we had to sit-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you ask, start again with when you passed your wife-- your wife's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when we-- when I pass my wife's family, so we had to sit on the floor, on the-- on the ground. We were all sitting on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is before the train? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was on a-- on the Plac, Plac Zgody, where they put all the people together. And from there, they-- they were moving to the trains. And we were sitting there for a few hours on-- on a-- on the floor-- on the ground. And I mentioned to my parents, to my family, that I will escape. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father said, look, you are young, boy. If you can do it, do it. After a few hours, they ask all people-- there-- there was order to get up from the floor and to march.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we marched toward the train. I would say, approximately two, three miles. During the march-- marching, I jump out from the row of people and jump out into-- jump in-- into a house. You had to-- you marched out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out-- out of the ghetto, and on the streets of-- of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of Cracow, going into the trains. I jump out of the-- of the row and jump into a door of the house. But I was caught by some SS men and brought back to the-- to the column of the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was lucky that he didn't shoot me then, right there. But, obviously, he told himself, why should I shoot him now? He's going to go anyhow. So I was pressing myself between people to reach my parents, because it took a few minutes, away, and my parents were already in front. So I reached them. And we came to the station. And we were wait-- waiting until they opened the doors. And after an hour or two hours later, the order came to open the doors. And we had to go in and jump in on the-- into the cattle trucks-- cars, cattle cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this had to be done fast, because they were beating. We with-- with anything they had in their hand, they were beating people, they should-- to move as fast as possible. When I jump in on to-- to the truck, to the cattle car, I occupied the window. And my parents came. My brother helped my parents. They came up into the cattle car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to my father, you see the little window? I'm going to jump out of the window. I'm going to cut the bars off, and I'm going on a jump.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here again, my father said, do it, whatever you think is right. [SOBBING] So, as we were staying and the doors were closed in the cattle cars, people became very hot. They start to undress themselves. Matter of fact, I undressed myself, too. Not-- not bottomless but topless. And people were start to dying. And children were dying. Afterward, the train start to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember how many people were in each-- in the car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around a few hundred.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are bit like the regular cars, the freight cars that they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Freight cars, right. 400, 500 people. And when the train start to roll, I took out a blade, hacksaw blade, what I always carry in my boots. I-- I wore boots, high boot. I took a-- a hacksaw blade, and I start to cut the bars. And some people, inside, start to scream, telling me that if-- wherever we going to come, and they're going to count us up, they would see that there's not enough people what we were. That they were going to kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked them, are you think that you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that you are going to live? Don't you see where you're going? [SOBBING] No-- nobody believed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Minutes later, I broke the bars, and I said goodbye to my parents. I even asked my sister to jump. [SOBBING] But she didn't want it. She wanted to stay with-- with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother picked me up. I got up with the feet out, then jumped off the train. And he's supposed to jump later, a few minutes later.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made ourselves a agreement-- agreement, that when you jump, I going to go forwards, and he's going to go backwards, so-- [SOBBING] we can-- so we could meet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But when I jump, I lost consciousness. And I was lying on the tracks through the whole night. Lucky that no train came through. And when I woke up, it was already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] morning, early in the morning. It was early in the morning. And I start to walk forwards, to see if I going to meet my brother. No way. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SOBBING] So I went. I knew the territory very well. So I went walking to a place named Klaj. And there was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] train going through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went into the train and went to Bochnia. It was a little bigger town than those little towns. [BACKGROUND NOISE] There, in Bochnia, there was a ghetto. And I smuggled myself into the ghetto. I was in Bochnia for two or three days, no more. Because I had the feeling that something is going to happen in Bochnia, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So early in the morning, after two, three days, I left the ghetto in Bochnia, went back to the train, and went-- You were able to get false papers, is that it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a false paper. I had a false paper. I had a false paper with me. And I went back. I-- I wasn't wearing the armband. I went back to the train and went back to Cracow. In Cracow, I had a very good friend, who was not Jewish. He was a Christian. I came to his house. And he said, all right, you can stay with me for a few days. But ghetto still exist. They didn't move everybody out. When the announcement was that-- that ghetto going to be liquidated, people were running. So they couldn't-- they couldn't transport the whole-- the whole ghetto. So they transport some of the people, maybe 20,000 people. And we were the people in that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to this Polish man, whom I knew. And he said, all right, you can stay with me a few days. I stood there for a few days, walking out on the street. And as I was walking out from his house, I saw a guy standing across the street, through two or three days, always the same guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was afraid that something is going on, that somebody know that I'm Jewish or something. And I'm outside the ghetto. So I decided to go back into the ghetto. I came to-- close to the ghetto, and I smuggled myself into the ghetto. People knew. Some people knew, who knew me, that I was sent out with the-- with the group. So they used to come to me, ask me, what happened? Where were you? What did you do? How did you come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to my wife's apartment, where she was living with her father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had the youngest brother, like I told you. He was living with some Irish people-- not Irish, only Christian people. What my father paid for it. They should to keep him. But between the time what we were sent on the transport, he saw that, no, he doesn't get no-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how would I say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Money?

speaker: interviewee
text: --he don't get no connection with us. So he decided that he's going to come to ghetto, too. He was then 14 1/2 years old, about 15 years old. So when he came in, he say-- he say, my gosh, what did you do? So he asked, [SOBBING] where-- where are the parents? [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SOBBING] So I had to tell him where-- where the parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I left the-- the train, my father gave me a gold watch. The gold watch, I decide to-- to sell, because I knew there is a company, name by Kabel, and they are holding Jews as workers, young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead of me, I-- or my brother, we tried to send the-- the youngest brother there to-- to-- to this place, to the company. And we got him in. Which way? I came to one guy, and I told him, I would like him to help me out with my brother, to go to Kabel and work there. So he said, OK, I can help you. But it is going to cost you money. So I said, don't worry about it. I will pay for it. So, like I told you before, I sold this watch. We made a date. I paid the money. And he went to Kabel. And he was in Kabel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was with-- when I came back, I was without no papers. So I decided-- there was no decision. There was announcement that they need young people, strong people to build a concentration camp, Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I volunteered. I didn't have no papers, so I volunteered. This volunteer kept me-- kept me alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were going-- going maybe five, six miles to Plaszow, from ghetto, every day, to build barracks there. When we built first three barracks, they didn't let us go to ghetto. We stood right in Plaszow. And this name was Concentration Camp Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So as long as I was coming back to ghetto, I saw my wife. But after that, I lost completely connection with-- with ghetto in March 13. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to Plaszow on October. October 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: '43. And Plaszow ghetto-- no, October '42. Plaszow ghetto was liquidated October-- March 13, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a ghetto in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the Cracow ghetto was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto was liquidated March 13. So I was in Plaszow from October to March. So just a group of people who-- barackenbau. They called it barackenbau. And we were building the barracks. On March 13, the whole-- No, let me-- let me come back a few days before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Already then, when I was going into the ghetto to sleep and-- and working in Plaszow as a barackenbau, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was-- the commander of the-- of the camp was by the name Amon Goeth. You probably know the name, because it's very known, very known name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By him, a person was nothing, like a-- like a fly. If he saw that somebody didn't work, he took a gun and he was-- he was shot. Some-- sometimes, even if somebody was working, he shot that man, how he wanted to. This Amon Goeth, he brought some people into the ghetto-- into the Plaszow, into the Plaszow, somewhere around 5,000 or 6,000 people into the Plaszow. And the rest was staying in ghetto yet. The ghetto wasn't liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: These people came from Cracow, from the Cracow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto into the Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some stayed in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some stay a little longer. And the traffic was between the ghetto and Plaszow. People from Plaszow used to go to work to ghetto. Because still, in Paszow, was not-- no work to tend. There was no-- no factories made, only some sleeping barracks. And two girls didn't-- two women, girls, young girls didn't come back for one night, didn't come back to Plaszow. And like, you know about it, that every morning we had appells. And they count people, how many people there were. And if somebody was missing, they had to look for them. On one of those days, on the appell, the Jewish policemen, two Jewish policemen, who were there, in Plaszow, already, came to Goeth, because he-- always, by appell, he was present, and told them that they are missing two people. And they know that they are missing girls, because boy-- men were separate and women were separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave the order to look for the girls. And they didn't find those girls. So he took those two policemen and shot them right there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because they were responsible for-- for-- for the group what was upset-- up in ghetto-- up in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was present by it. And you wouldn't even believe what hardship you going through if you see people dying on the floor, and you had to march and look on it. So you have to-- he was putting in your mind, this is the way you're going to look when you're going to run. On March 13, the whole ghetto was moved into the Plaszow. And then a man, by the name Hilovich, was the a lageralteste. This means the chief of the police, Jewish police. And he found himself some people. And the whole concentration camp was filled up. The ghetto was liquidated. And everybody was in-- in-- in the concentration camp. Now, here is another-- the same story. Now that transportation were-- transport were going out and other Jews were coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same as it was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what was the-- what were the duties of the Jewish police in the ghetto-- in the-- in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. The same what was in Poland, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: They kept order?

speaker: interviewee
text: The orders, and they kept counting. And they-- when the appells were, they were counting people. They were giving a report to the-- Goeth, who was the camp commandant. And he was one who, your life was depend on him. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get special treatment, the Yiddishe Polizei?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, they get better treatment than-- than we had. They used to sleep in their own barracks, and they had beds. And we had the bleachers. And they had food. And we didn't have. We were waiting until something that we got to eat. After work, we got some soup, some-- a piece of bread, and that's it. And I was in this concentration camp, Plaszow, until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother, the youngest brother-- factory closed up, and all Jews from this factory came out to-- to Plaszow. And my youngest brother came with them. And a few days later, he was [GERMAN] again to a vernichtungslagers, others, like Belzec, like other-- other camps. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew about the vernichtungslager, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, we knew. We knew-- then, we knew everything. Then-- then they--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in-- this was the end of 1943, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was in March, 1940-- March-- between March and May, 1943, when-- when the ghetto was liquidated, we already knew that. When I jumped the train, I already knew that this-- this train, those people who were in this train, didn't go to-- to work. Only went right straight into oven. And probably half of them were dead already in the cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 19-- 1943, in Plaszow, I was working as an electrician. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I changed my trade. Before, I was an auto mechanic, then I was electrician.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would-- I was working electrician. I was wearing a belt, all kind of tools around. So I had the permission to walk around the camp. If somebody stopped me, I was electrician, go fix the wires, electric wires, this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had the-- the German supervision, what you were doing?
